invariant_definition: The cessation of bondage to *saṃsāra* — the end of the cycle of rebirth and the attainment of the soul's highest state. All Vedāntic schools agree (a) that *mokṣa* is the *paramapuruṣārtha* (the highest of the four human goals); (b) that it is achieved by *brahma-jñāna* in some form (with *karma* preparatory but never of itself sufficient); (c) that the *Brahma-Sūtra*'s fourth *adhyāya* (*phala-adhyāya*) treats it. Schools diverge on the content of liberation: identity with *brahman* (Advaita), eternal blissful service to the Lord with retained individuality (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, Acintya-Bhedābheda); on whether liberation occurs in the body (*jīvanmukti*, Advaita) or only after it (*videhamukti*, Dvaita disputes *jīvanmukti*); and on whether all four classical *mukti*-types (*sālokya*, *sāmīpya*, *sārūpya*, *sāyujya*) are real options, or only some.

per_school.definition: *Mokṣa* is the realization of *ātman*'s pre-existing identity with *brahman*. Nothing is gained or attained; *avidyā* is removed and what already was is recognized. *Jīvanmukti* is admitted: the realized one lives out *prārabdha-karma* in a body whose phenomenality has ceased to bind. Final *videhamukti* is the falling-away of even the *prārabdha*-residue at the body's death. *Mokṣa* is *nityaṃ siddham* — eternally accomplished — not *kāryam* (a thing-to-be-produced).

*Mokṣa* is the soul's reaching Vaikuṇṭha (the Lord's transcendent abode) after death, where it enjoys eternal *bhoga* of *brahman* in the four *mukti*-modes (*sālokya*, *sāmīpya*, *sārūpya*, *sāyujya*) — *sāyujya* understood not as identity but as fullest intimacy. The *jīva*'s individuality is retained eternally; *dharmabhūta-jñāna* is fully expanded and unobstructed. *Jīvanmukti* in the strong Advaita sense is denied; what is admitted is *prapanna* (one who has surrendered) living out the body in unbroken devotion.

*Mokṣa* is the soul's eternal blissful service to Viṣṇu in Vaikuṇṭha. Crucially, the bliss enjoyed is *graded* (*ānanda-taratamya*) — each soul enjoys the *ānanda* proportional to its intrinsic nature; equality of liberated souls is denied. Only *mukti-yogya* souls reach *mokṣa*; *nitya-saṃsārī* souls cycle eternally; *tamo-yogya* souls reach the *andha-tamas* (eternal hell). *Jīvanmukti* is rejected as incoherent: liberation requires the dropping of the *karma*-body.

*Mokṣa* in the impersonal Advaita sense (*sāyujya* as merger) is treated as low or even spiritually undesirable; the goal is *prema-bhakti* — eternal loving service to Kṛṣṇa in one of the five *rasas* in Goloka. The four classical *muktis* are accepted as graded options below *prema-bhakti*; *prema-bhakti* itself is not classified as one of them but stands above them.

*Mokṣa* is *pratyabhijñā* — recognition of one's identity with Śiva, the recognition that one *was already* Śiva. It is achievable in life; the realized one (*siddha*) lives in *jagad-ānanda* — the cosmic bliss in which the world is no longer over against him but is his own self-expression.

translator_note: 'Liberation' is acceptable as a gloss but obscures that the schools disagree on what one is liberated *into*. Translating *mokṣa* uniformly as 'liberation' across schools is fine if the reader understands that the post-liberation state varies from absolute identity (Advaita) to eternal blissful service (Viśiṣṇu schools) to recognized cosmic sovereignty (Trika).
